title: When an Executor Needs to Hire Professional Help
summary: Many executors handle simple estates on their own, but certain situations genuinely call for professional support.
category: After a Loss III: Estate & Digital
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: When an Executor Needs to Hire Professional Help | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Many executors handle simple estates on their own, but certain situations genuinely call for professional support.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Complex, high-value, or contested estates usually benefit from an attorney.
An accountant may be needed for complicated tax situations or ongoing business interests.
Professional fees are typically paid from the estate, not the executor personally.

paragraphs: Estates that are large, involve significant debt, include a business, span multiple states, or face any disagreement among beneficiaries are strong candidates for professional legal help, even if the will itself seems straightforward.

An accountant can be valuable for complex final tax returns, especially if the estate includes investment property, business income, or requires ongoing tax filings during a lengthy probate — a cost that comes from the estate's funds, not the executor's own pocket.

nextSteps: Recognize early warning signs that a situation is more complex than expected.
Consult a probate attorney if debt, disputes, or multi-state assets are involved.
Remember that professional fees are typically an estate expense, not a personal one.
disclaimer: General guidance — a probate attorney can give a clearer sense of whether your specific estate needs professional support.
